  • Hide All Code

ERT Pilot — Exploratory Results

False Cardiac Feedback Paradigm — By-subject aggregation

Guido Maria Caruso

2026-04-08

library(tidyverse)
library(ez)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(ggplot2)

Overview

This report presents exploratory analyses of pilot data from the ERT task (Emotion Recognition Task with False Cardiac Feedback).

Paradigm: Participants rated the emotional intensity of facial expressions (happiness, anger, fear) while receiving pulsatile somatosensory feedback at rates faster, slower, or synchronous with their actual heart rate. The paradigm is based on Patchitt et al. (2025, Scientific Reports).

Key hypothesis: False feedback (faster or slower than veridical HR) should progressively bias emotional intensity ratings over the course of each stimulation block — an exposure effect reflecting interoceptive embodiment.

Analytical approach:

  • Raw trial data aggregated to participant means per condition × valence cell
  • Repeated-measures ANOVA (Type III, ezANOVA) on these means
  • All degrees of freedom are at the participant level (conservative)
  • Within-subject factors: condition (synchronous / faster / slower) × valence (positive / negative)
  • Valence: positive = happiness; negative = anger + fear (collapsed, following Patchitt et al.)
# Load and merge all *_FULL.csv files from the working directory
# Each file = one participant, 360 main trials
df_raw <- list.files(path = ".", pattern = "FULL\\.csv$", full.names = TRUE) |>
  map_dfr(read_csv, show_col_types = FALSE,
          col_types = cols(participant_id = col_character())) |>
  filter(segment_type == "main") |>
  mutate(
    participant_id = as.factor(participant_id),
    condition      = factor(condition,
                            levels = c("synchronous", "faster", "slower")),
    valence        = factor(ifelse(emotion == "happiness", "positive", "negative"),
                            levels = c("positive", "negative")),
    vas_value      = as.numeric(vas_value),
    rt             = as.numeric(rt)
  )

# Aggregate to one mean per participant x condition x valence
df <- df_raw |>
  group_by(participant_id, condition, valence) |>
  summarise(
    vas_m = mean(vas_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    rt_m  = mean(rt, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

N participants: 14
N trials (raw): 5040
Rows after aggregation: 84 (14 participants × 3 conditions × 2 valences)

1. Descriptives

Participant-level means (M) and standard deviations (SD) for VAS and RT across conditions and valences.

VAS by condition × valence

df |>
  group_by(condition, valence) |>
  summarise(M  = round(mean(vas_m), 2),
            SD = round(sd(vas_m), 2),
            n  = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  kable(caption = "Mean VAS per condition x valence (participant means)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Mean VAS per condition x valence (participant means)
condition valence M SD n
synchronous positive 21.18 7.79 14
synchronous negative -28.56 12.32 14
faster positive 20.21 9.69 14
faster negative -28.15 11.45 14
slower positive 20.57 7.98 14
slower negative -28.79 11.37 14

RT by condition × valence

df |>
  group_by(condition, valence) |>
  summarise(M  = round(mean(rt_m), 3),
            SD = round(sd(rt_m), 3),
            n  = n(), .groups = "drop") |>
  kable(caption = "Mean RT per condition x valence (participant means)") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Mean RT per condition x valence (participant means)
condition valence M SD n
synchronous positive 1.569 0.578 14
synchronous negative 1.578 0.543 14
faster positive 1.432 0.430 14
faster negative 1.366 0.454 14
slower positive 1.521 0.467 14
slower negative 1.429 0.483 14

2. Sanity check — intensity × VAS

VAS ratings should correlate strongly with stimulus intensity within each valence: positive faces should yield higher ratings at higher intensities, negative faces lower ratings. Failure to replicate this would indicate that participants are not using the scale as intended.

df_raw |>
  mutate(intensity = as.numeric(intensity)) |>
  group_by(valence) |>
  summarise(
    rho = round(cor(intensity, vas_value, method = "spearman"), 3),
    p   = case_when(
      cor.test(intensity, vas_value, method = "spearman")$p.value < 0.001 ~ "< .001",
      TRUE ~ sprintf("%.4f",
                     cor.test(intensity, vas_value, method = "spearman")$p.value)
    ),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  kable(caption = "Spearman correlation: stimulus intensity ~ VAS rating") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Spearman correlation: stimulus intensity ~ VAS rating
valence rho p
positive 0.723 < .001
negative -0.598 < .001

3. rmANOVA — VAS

Repeated-measures ANOVA on participant-level VAS means. The key test is whether condition (faster / slower vs synchronous) or its interaction with valence reaches significance.

Note: no main effect of condition is expected at this sample size — consistent with Patchitt et al. (2025), who also found no average effect of condition (F = 1.31, p = .274) in their initial analysis. The exposure effect is expected to emerge in the time-series analysis (Section 6).

anova_vas <- ezANOVA(
  data     = df,
  dv       = vas_m,
  wid      = participant_id,
  within   = .(condition, valence),
  type     = 3,
  detailed = TRUE
)
bold_ezanova(anova_vas)
Effect DFn DFd SSn SSd F p p<.05 ges
condition 2 26 2.506 519.574 0.0627 0.9394 0.0003
valence 1 13 50746.099 5091.382 129.5718 < .001
0.8610
condition:valence 2 26 7.067 353.652 0.2598 0.7732 0.0009

Bold rows = p < .05. ges = generalised eta squared (effect size).

Sphericity corrections (Greenhouse-Geisser)

Mauchly’s test is reported for effects with more than 2 levels. If sphericity is violated (p < .05), use the GG-corrected p-value.

anova_vas$`Sphericity Corrections` |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 4))) |>
  kable() |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Effect GGe p[GG] p[GG]<.05 HFe p[HF] p[HF]<.05
2 condition 0.9339 0.9298 1.0846 0.9394
4 condition:valence 0.8590 0.7404 0.9772 0.7683

4. rmANOVA — RT

Reaction times may reflect decision certainty: if false feedback increases perceptual ambiguity, participants may respond more slowly in faster/slower conditions compared to synchronous.

anova_rt <- ezANOVA(
  data     = df,
  dv       = rt_m,
  wid      = participant_id,
  within   = .(condition, valence),
  type     = 3,
  detailed = TRUE
)
bold_ezanova(anova_rt)
Effect DFn DFd SSn SSd F p p<.05 ges
condition 2 26 0.428 1.768 3.1495 0.0596 0.0219
valence 1 13 0.052 0.504 1.3309 0.2694 0.0027
condition:valence 2 26 0.037 0.499 0.9762 0.3901 0.0020

Bold rows = p < .05. ges = generalised eta squared (effect size).

Sphericity corrections (Greenhouse-Geisser)

anova_rt$`Sphericity Corrections` |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 4))) |>
  kable() |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"), full_width = FALSE)
Effect GGe p[GG] p[GG]<.05 HFe p[HF] p[HF]<.05
2 condition 0.7469 0.0774 0.8218 0.0717
4 condition:valence 0.7348 0.3697 0.8055 0.3760

5. Visualisations

VAS per condition × valence

Each dot represents one participant’s mean VAS. The crossbar shows the grand mean across participants.

df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = condition, y = vas_m, fill = condition)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.4, trim = FALSE) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "crossbar", width = 0.4,
               color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ valence) +
  labs(title = "VAS per condition x valence (participant means)",
       x = "Condition", y = "Mean VAS",
       caption = "Crossbar = grand mean; dots = individual participants") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

RT per condition × valence

df |>
  ggplot(aes(x = condition, y = rt_m, fill = condition)) +
  geom_violin(alpha = 0.4, trim = FALSE) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.1, size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +
  stat_summary(fun = mean, geom = "crossbar", width = 0.4,
               color = "black", linewidth = 0.8) +
  facet_wrap(~ valence) +
  labs(title = "RT per condition x valence (participant means)",
       x = "Condition", y = "Mean RT (s)",
       caption = "Crossbar = grand mean; dots = individual participants") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")

6. Exposure effect — time series within block

Following Patchitt et al. (2025), we examined whether the effect of false feedback develops progressively over the course of each stimulation block. Trial position within the block (1–6) captures this exposure effect: early trials have had less exposure to the feedback signal, while later trials have been exposed longer.

Data are aggregated to participant means per condition × valence × trial position, keeping all degrees of freedom at the participant level.

df_ts <- df_raw |>
  mutate(trial_in_mini = as.factor(trial_in_mini)) |>
  group_by(participant_id, condition, valence, trial_in_mini) |>
  summarise(
    vas_m = mean(vas_value, na.rm = TRUE),
    rt_m  = mean(rt, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

cat("Rows after aggregation:", nrow(df_ts),
    "=", n_distinct(df_ts$participant_id),
    "participants x 3 conditions x 2 valences x 6 trial positions\n")
## Rows after aggregation: 504 = 14 participants x 3 conditions x 2 valences x 6 trial positions

rmANOVA — VAS with trial position

The key effect of interest is the condition × valence × trial_in_mini interaction, which in Patchitt et al. (2025) was F(3) = 47.87, p < .001.

anova_vas_ts <- ezANOVA(
  data   = df_ts,
  dv     = vas_m,
  wid    = participant_id,
  within = .(condition, valence, trial_in_mini),
  type   = 3,
  detailed = TRUE
)
bold_ezanova(anova_vas_ts)
Effect DFn DFd SSn SSd F p p<.05 ges
condition 2 26 48.213 2855.846 0.2195 0.8044 0.0005
valence 1 13 300870.550 29708.450 131.6567 < .001
0.7671
trial_in_mini 5 65 565.849 7401.219 0.9939 0.4285 0.0062
condition:valence 2 26 32.842 2234.873 0.1910 0.8272 0.0004
condition:trial_in_mini 10 130 1300.205 12294.610 1.3748 0.1989 0.0140
valence:trial_in_mini 5 65 379.523 8862.757 0.5567 0.7327 0.0041
condition:valence:trial_in_mini 10 130 1923.925 14832.577 1.6862 0.0905 0.0206

rmANOVA — RT with trial position

anova_rt_ts <- ezANOVA(
  data   = df_ts,
  dv     = rt_m,
  wid    = participant_id,
  within = .(condition, valence, trial_in_mini),
  type   = 3,
  detailed = TRUE
)
bold_ezanova(anova_rt_ts)
Effect DFn DFd SSn SSd F p p<.05 ges
condition 2 26 2.717 10.080 3.5041 0.0449
0.0179
valence 1 13 0.449 2.522 2.3155 0.1520 0.0030
trial_in_mini 5 65 1.137 8.506 1.7374 0.1386 0.0075
condition:valence 2 26 0.144 3.949 0.4746 0.6274 0.0010
condition:trial_in_mini 10 130 1.054 11.888 1.1523 0.3290 0.0070
valence:trial_in_mini 5 65 0.525 8.232 0.8291 0.5337 0.0035
condition:valence:trial_in_mini 10 130 0.614 8.712 0.9164 0.5203 0.0041

Plot — VAS exposure effect (replica of Patchitt et al. Fig. 3)

If false feedback is being embodied as an interoceptive arousal signal, VAS ratings should diverge across conditions over the course of each block — with faster FFB amplifying ratings and slower FFB attenuating them.

df_ts |>
  group_by(condition, valence, trial_in_mini) |>
  summarise(M  = mean(vas_m),
            SE = sd(vas_m) / sqrt(n()),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = as.numeric(as.character(trial_in_mini)),
             y = M, color = condition, group = condition)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = M - SE, ymax = M + SE, fill = condition),
              alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ valence, labeller = labeller(
    valence = c(positive = "Positive faces (happiness)",
                negative = "Negative faces (anger + fear)"))) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:6) +
  labs(title = "Exposure effect of FFB on VAS — participant means",
       subtitle = "Replica of Patchitt et al. (2025) Fig. 3",
       x = "Trial position in block (1–6)",
       y = "Mean VAS ± SE",
       color = "Condition", fill = "Condition") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)

Plot — RT exposure effect

df_ts |>
  group_by(condition, valence, trial_in_mini) |>
  summarise(M  = mean(rt_m),
            SE = sd(rt_m) / sqrt(n()),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = as.numeric(as.character(trial_in_mini)),
             y = M, color = condition, group = condition)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = M - SE, ymax = M + SE, fill = condition),
              alpha = 0.15, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  facet_wrap(~ valence, labeller = labeller(
    valence = c(positive = "Positive faces (happiness)",
                negative = "Negative faces (anger + fear)"))) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:6) +
  labs(title = "RT over trial position within block — participant means",
       x = "Trial position in block (1–6)",
       y = "Mean RT ± SE (s)",
       color = "Condition", fill = "Condition") +
  theme_bw(base_size = 13)

7. Summary

Key results across all analyses. Bold = significant at p < .05.

Analysis Effect F df p
VAS condition 0.06 2, 26 0.939
VAS valence 129.57 1, 13 < .001
VAS condition × valence 0.26 2, 26 0.773
RT condition 3.15 2, 26 0.060
RT valence 1.33 1, 13 0.269
RT condition × valence 0.98 2, 26 0.390

Notes:

  • No main effect of condition on VAS is expected at this sample size — consistent with Patchitt et al. (2025).
  • The valence effect on VAS confirms task validity: participants correctly distinguish positive from negative faces.
  • The exposure effect (condition × valence × trial position) is the primary hypothesis test and will be evaluated at full sample (N = 50).

Report generated automatically from raw *_FULL.csv files.
2026-04-08 13:13
